retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Cytochrome P450 -- Clinical Significance -- CYP2D6:
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibitors: bupropion, duloxetine, fluoxetine, paroxetine, quinidine, ritonavir, sertraline, terbinafine Inducers: none Substrates: amitriptyline, carvedilol, codeine, dextromethorphan, diltiazem, donepezil, haloperidol, metoprolol, nifedipine, ondansetron, oxycodone, propranolol, risperidone, tamoxifen, tramadol

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Cytochrome P450 -- Clinical Significance -- CYP3A4:
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibitors: amiodarone, amitriptyline, aprepitant, carvedilol, chloramphenicol, cimetidine, ciprofloxacin, clarithromycin, codeine, donepezil, fluvoxamine, haloperidol, imatinib, ketoconazole, metoprolol, paroxetine, risperidone, ritonavir, tramadol, verapamil Inducers: carbamazepine, griseofulvin, phenobarbital, phenytoin, rifampin, St. John’s wort Substrates: alprazolam, amlodipine, buspirone, calcium channel blockers, caffeine, citalopram, clopidogrel, cocaine, cyclosporine, diazepam, erythromycin, estradiol, lidocaine, losartan, many chemotherapeutic drugs, montelukast, quetiapine, sertraline, sildenafil, statin drugs, tacrolimus, warfarin, zolpidem

retrieved_docs.title: Thioridazine -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: There is a black-box warning for dementia-related psychosis because it may increase the risk of death secondary to cerebrovascular events in the elderly. Thioridazine is associated with a prolonged QT interval. Thus patients with long QT syndrome, QT intervals prolonged to more than 450, or taking concomitant medications known to prolong the QT interval should avoid thioridazine. Also, patients with known heart arrhythmias or hypotensive or hypertensive heart disease should also avoid this medication. Because of drug-drug interactions, the clinician should be aware of the patient's medication profile. Thioridazine is metabolized by the hepatic enzyme cytochrome P450 CYP450 2D6. Medications that inhibit CYP450 2D6 should be used with caution when prescribing thioridazine. Medications that may affect thioridazine levels include but are not limited to fluoxetine, paroxetine, duloxetine, fluvoxamine, propranolol, citalopram, and pindolol. Another rare but serious side effect of thioridazine is agranulocytosis. Each patient should be evaluated for abnormal white blood cell counts pre and post-administration of thioridazine. [11] Caution is advised in pregnant patients during the third trimester, although teratogenicity is not expected. For breastfeeding, it is recommended to use an alternative agent as there is no data in humans to assess the risk in breast milk.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Biotransformation -- Clinical Significance -- CYP2D6
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibitors: Isoniazid, ketoconazole, methadone, nicardipine

retrieved_docs.title: Metoprolol -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Metabolism: Metoprolol is extensively metabolized in the liver by the cytochrome P450 enzyme CYP2D6. [24] Then it undergoes oxidative deamination and O-demethylation to form several inactive metabolites, such as alpha-hydroxy-metoprolol and 4’-hydroxy-metoprolol. Metoprolol is a racemic mixture of R- and S-enantiomers with different pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic properties. The R-enantiomer is more potent and selective for β-1 receptors than the S-enantiomer; it is also more prone to metabolism by CYP2D6. The S-enantiomer has a longer half-life and higher plasma concentration than the R-enantiomer. The metabolism of metoprolol is influenced by genetic polymorphism of CYP2D6, which can result in poor, intermediate, extensive, or ultra-rapid metabolizers. Poor metabolizers have higher plasma levels and longer half-lives of metoprolol than extensive ones, while ultra-rapid metabolizers have lower plasma levels and shorter half-lives than extensive ones. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Distribution -- Clinical Significance -- Drug: amiodarone
retrieved_docs.content: CYP enzyme: CYP2C9 and CYP3A4 inhibitor Drug-Drug Interaction: warfarin Metabolizing Enzyme: CYP2C9 Side Effects: Increased risk of bleeding caused by increased warfarin level. Drug: carbamazepine , phenobarbital , phenytoin CYP enzyme: CYP3A4 inducer Drug-Drug Interaction: Ethinyl estradiol-containing contraceptives Metabolizing Enzyme: CYP3A4 Side Effects: Unplanned pregnancy caused by reduced estradiol level. Drug: clarithromycin , erythromycin , telithromycin CYP enzyme: CYP3A4 inhibitor Drug-Drug Interaction: simvastatin, verapamil Metabolizing Enzyme: CYP3A4 Side Effects: Myopathy or rhabdomyolysis caused by increased simvastatin level. Hypotension and QT interval prolongation caused by increased verapamil level. Drug: diltiazem , verapamil CYP enzyme: CYP3A4 inhibitor Drug-Drug Interaction: prednisone Metabolizing Enzyme: CYP3A4 Side Effects: Immunosuppression caused by increased prednisolone serum levels. Drug: fluoxetine , paroxetine CYP enzyme: CYP2D6 inhibitor

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Cytochrome P450 -- Clinical Significance -- CYP2C9:
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibitors: amiodarone, fluconazole, fluoxetine, metronidazole, ritonavir, trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole Inducers: carbamazepine, phenobarbital, phenytoin, rifampin Substrates: carvedilol, celecoxib, glipizide, ibuprofen, irbesartan, losartan, cannabinoids

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacogenomics Overview -- Clinical Significance -- Cytochrome P450 Gene Polymorphism in Drug Metabolism
retrieved_docs.content: Variations in CYP2D6 can impact the metabolism of opioids, antidepressants, and antipsychotics. Atomoxetine, a selective serotonin/norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor, undergoes hepatic metabolism that is subject to CYP2D6 polymorphism. The physiologically based pharmacokinetic features of atomoxetine differ among various CYP2D6 genotypes. [17] Therefore, the starting dose needs to be reduced in poor metabolizers to prevent elevated serum drug levels. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Drug Distribution -- Clinical Significance -- CYP2D6
retrieved_docs.content: Inhibitors: amiodarone, cimetidine, diphenhydramine, fluoxetine, paroxetine, quinidine, ritonavir, terbinafine Inducers: none Substrates: amitriptyline, carvedilol, codeine, donepezil, haloperidol, metoprolol, paroxetine, risperidone, tramadol

retrieved_docs.title: Mirabegron -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: The liver’s CYP450 system predominantly metabolizes mirabegron. Monitoring and dose adjustment should occur with patients on other medications with a narrow therapeutic index and also undergo metabolism via CYP2D6. [13] According to American Family Physicians (AAFP), potent inhibitors of the CYP2D6 system are amiodarone, diphenhydramine, fluoxetine, paroxetine, quinidine, ritonavir, and terbinafine. Substrates of the CYP2D6 system are amitriptyline, carvedilol, codeine, donepezil, haloperidol, metoprolol, paroxetine, risperidone, and tramadol. There are no significant inducers. In this light, it is vital to consider all comorbidities and drugs patients are on to prevent undesirable drug-drug interactions. It is recommended to take a thorough history, considering past medical history and review of medications before starting mirabegron therapy. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Carvedilol -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Metabolism: Carvedilol undergoes hepatic metabolism, initially through oxidation, followed by glucuronidation and conjugation. The primary mediators of its metabolism are cytochromes P450, specifically CYP2D6 and CYP2C9. In addition, carvedilol acts as an inhibitor of P-glycoprotein. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Bisoprolol -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Abrupt discontinuation of clonidine while taking bisoprolol results in a catecholamine surge and can lead to rebound hypertension. Bisoprolol should be stopped several days before the discontinuation of clonidine. [34] Concomitant use of bisoprolol with calcium channel blockers such as verapamil and diltiazem may worsen the myocardial function and result in hypotension, bradycardia, and AV block. [35] Strong CYP3A4 induces, like rifampin, increases the clearance of bisoprolol, resulting in a short elimination half-life of bisoprolol. [36] Concomitant administration of cholinesterase inhibitors like donepezil with bisoprolol can increase the risk for bradycardia, falls, and syncope. [37] Concomitant use of bisoprolol with digoxin delays AV conduction and can increase the risk of bradycardia. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Donepezil -- Adverse Effects -- Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Donepezil exhibits synergistic effects when combined with other cholinesterase-blocking agents such as neostigmine and physostigmine. Donepezil can potentially prolong the effects of depolarizing neuromuscular blocking agents such as suxamethonium. [22] Donepezil may elevate the risk of bradycardia when used concurrently with beta-blockers such as carvedilol, metoprolol, atenolol, and propranolol. [23] CYP2D6 and CYP3A4 inducers, such as phenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital, rifampin, and dexamethasone, have the potential to reduce donepezil levels by increasing their elimination rate. [24] Theoretically, inhibitors of CYP3A4 and CYP2D6, such as ketoconazole and quinidine, can potentially inhibit the metabolism of donepezil. However, the clinical significance of this interaction is currently unknown and requires further investigation. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Carvedilol -- Adverse Effects -- Drug-Drug Interactions
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodarone: Amiodarone and its metabolite inhibit CYP2C9 and P-glycoprotein, significantly increasing the concentration of carvedilol's S (-) enantiomer. This heightened concentration may enhance beta-blocking activity and potentially reduce heart rate or slow cardiac conduction. Furthermore, it is imperative to closely monitor for bradycardia or heart block, especially when coadministering amiodarone with carvedilol.

retrieved_docs.title: Atenolol -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Older patients: According to ACC/AHA guidelines, in older patients, the accumulation of renally cleared drugs such as angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors (ACEi), atenolol, sotalol, and nadolol is influenced by reduced glomerular filtration, renal tubular function, and renal blood flow. Initiating atenolol is advised at a lower dose in this population. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Almotriptan -- Contraindications -- Box Warnings
retrieved_docs.content: Minor almotriptan clearance occurs through the CYP450 system (specifically, CYP3A4); therefore, the clinician should avoid other drugs metabolized through this pathway because they may decrease almotriptan clearance. These drugs include but are not limited to verapamil, nifedipine, losartan, cyclosporine, and moclobemide. [15] Patients with reduced enzyme activity should be cautious in dosing the drug.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2202
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodarone exhibits a mechanism of action that aligns with the pharmacologic profile of adrenergic beta‑antagonists, indicating that its therapeutic effects involve beta‑adrenergic blockade. In addition, amiodarone functions as an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 enzyme CYP3A, classifying it as an inhibitory cytochrome P450 CYP3A agent. This combination of beta‑antagonist activity and CYP3A inhibition influences both its antiarrhythmic efficacy and its potential for drug‑drug interactions, making awareness of these properties essential for safe and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 911
retrieved_docs.content: Human cytochrome P450 3A4 protein metabolizes both mikonazol and amiodaron, indicating that these drugs are substrates of this major hepatic enzyme. In addition, both mikonazol and amiodaron act as inhibitors of inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP2C9 and inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP3A, meaning they can decrease the metabolic activity of these isoforms and potentially alter the clearance of other co‑administered medications that depend on these pathways. Furthermore, the mechanism of action of amiodaron includes adrenergic beta‑antagonist activity, as described by the Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists [MoA] relationship. Together, these pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic interactions highlight the importance of careful drug‑interaction monitoring and dose adjustment when mikonazol, amiodaron, or other CYP3A4, CYP2C9, or CYP3A substrates are used concurrently in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1667
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodaron acts as an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 enzymes CYP2C9 and CYP3A. By suppressing the activity of these metabolic pathways, amiodaron can alter the clearance of drugs that are substrates of CYP2C9 or CYP3A, increasing the risk of drug‑drug interactions. Recognizing amiodaron’s inhibitory effects on both CYP2C9 and CYP3A is essential for clinicians when selecting and dosing concomitant medications, to ensure safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 487
retrieved_docs.content: Cytochrome P450 3A4 protein (human) is an important drug‑metabolizing enzyme that catalyzes the biotransformation of both mikonazol and omeprazol. Amiodarone is also a substrate of cytochrome P450 3A4, indicating that this enzyme contributes to the clearance of amiodarone in patients. In addition to being metabolized, amiodarone functions as an inhibitor of several cytochrome P450 isoforms, including inhibitory cytochrome P450 CYP2C9 and inhibitory cytochrome P450 CYP3A, as well as broader inhibitory enzymes. The class of inhibitory enzymes encompasses drugs such as omeprazol and racecadotril, suggesting that these agents can suppress enzymatic activity. Racecadotril is linked to thiorfan through a mapping relationship, indicating a structural or functional derivation from thiorfan. Furthermore, inhibitory enzymes are categorized under the parent group “inhibitory protein synthesis,” reflecting that their inhibitory actions may involve modulation of protein production pathways. Together, these relationships highlight a network in which cytochrome P450 3A4 mediates drug metabolism, while amiodarone and certain other agents act as inhibitors of cytochrome P450 isoforms and related enzymatic systems, influencing drug‑drug interactions and therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2389
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodaron is both a substrate and an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 enzyme system. Specifically, amiodaron is metabolized by the human cytochrome P450 3A4 protein, an enzyme that metabolizes a wide range of chemicals and drugs. At the same time, amiodaron is classified as an inhibitory cytochrome P450 CYP3A agent, meaning it can suppress the activity of CYP3A enzymes. This dual role as a substrate of CYP3A4 and an inhibitor of CYP3A highlights the potential for amiodaron to affect the metabolism of other concurrently administered medications, making awareness of drug‑drug interactions essential in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1786
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodaron is an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 enzymes identified as inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP2C9 and inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP3A, and it also acts as a broader inhibitory enzymů. Omeprazol belongs to the same class of inhibitory enzymů. Because both amiodaron and omeprazol can inhibit key metabolic enzymes, they have the potential to alter the pharmacokinetics of other concurrently administered drugs, making awareness of these enzyme‑inhibiting properties important for safe patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1434
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenergic beta‑Antagonists [MoA] exert their pharmacologic effect by acting as antagonisté beta‑1‑adrenergních receptorů, meaning they block beta‑1 adrenergic receptors. Amiodaron shares this mechanism of action, as it also functions as a beta‑1‑adrenergic receptor antagonist among its multiple antiarrhythmic actions. In addition to its beta‑blocking activity, amiodaron is an inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP2C9 and an inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP3A, indicating that it suppresses the activity of both CYP2C9 and CYP3A enzymes. These inhibitory effects on major hepatic drug‑metabolizing enzymes contribute to amiodaron’s potential for drug‑drug interactions and are important considerations in patient care and medication management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 2776
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodaron and omeprazol are both substrates of the human cytochrome P450 3A4 protein, which functions as an enzyme that metabolizes chemicals and drugs. The cytochrome P450 3A4 enzyme therefore plays a key role in the biotransformation and clearance of both amiodaron and omeprazol, influencing their pharmacokinetics, potential drug‑drug interactions, and dosing considerations in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1934
retrieved_docs.content: Conivaptan functions through inhibition of the Cytochrome P450 3A4 enzyme, making it a CYP3A4 inhibitor. Both conivaptan and the beta‑blocker bisoprolol share a contraindication in patients who have urinary retention (انقطاع البول), indicating that the presence of urinary retention should preclude the use of either medication to avoid adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 823
retrieved_docs.content: The NCIt COVID‑19 Terminology includes the drug concepts amiodaron and omeprazol, indicating that both are relevant to considerations of COVID‑19 pharmacotherapy. Both amiodaron and omeprazol are metabolized by the human cytochrome P450 3A4 protein, an enzyme that processes many xenobiotics. In addition to being substrates of CYP3A4, amiodaron functions as an inhibitory enzymů (an enzyme inhibitor), and omeprazol is also classified as an inhibitory enzymů, reflecting their potential to interfere with the activity of metabolic enzymes. Racecadotril, which is mapped from the related compound thiorfan, is likewise an inhibitory enzymů, further illustrating a class of drugs that can modulate enzyme function. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of CYP3A4‑mediated metabolism and the inhibitory properties of amiodaron, omeprazol, and racecadotril in influencing drug‑drug interactions and therapeutic decision‑making, particularly in the context of COVID‑19 treatment strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1555
retrieved_docs.content: Human cytochrome P450 3A4 protein metabolizes both miconazole and amiodarone, indicating that these drugs are substrates of this major hepatic enzyme. In addition, both miconazole and amiodarone act as inhibitors of cytochrome P450 enzymes, specifically inhibiting CYP2C9 and the broader CYP3A family, which can lead to drug‑drug interaction potential when co‑administered with other CYP2C9 or CYP3A substrates. Amiodarone also possesses pharmacologic activity characteristic of adrenergic beta‑antagonists; it antagonizes β‑1‑adrenergic receptors, placing its mechanism of action within the class of beta‑blockers. This β‑1 antagonism contributes to amiodarone’s antiarrhythmic effects and aligns it with other agents that block β‑adrenergic signaling. Understanding that miconazole and amiodarone share metabolic pathways (CYP3A4) and inhibitory effects on CYP2C9 and CYP3A, while amiodarone additionally provides β‑1 blockade, is essential for anticipating interactions, optimizing dosing, and ensuring safe patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2278
retrieved_docs.content: Mikonazol and amiodaron are agents that act as inhibitors of the metabolic enzymes described as inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP2C9 and inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP3A. Both inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP2C9 and inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP3A play key roles in the biotransformation of a wide variety of drugs. By inhibiting these enzymes, mikonazol and amiodaron can decrease the metabolic clearance of other medications that are substrates of CYP2C9 or CYP3A, potentially leading to increased plasma concentrations and heightened risk of adverse effects. Recognizing the inhibitory effects of mikonazol and amiodaron on inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP2C9 and inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP3A is important for clinicians to anticipate and manage possible drug‑drug interactions in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2012
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodarone is an antiarrhythmic drug that functions as an inhibitor of the hepatic enzyme cytochrome P450 CYP2C9. By suppressing CYP2C9 activity, amiodarone can alter the metabolic clearance of other medications that are substrates of this enzyme, leading to increased plasma concentrations and a heightened risk of adverse effects. Awareness of amiodarone’s inhibitory effect on CYP2C9 is essential for clinicians when prescribing concomitant drugs metabolized by this pathway, in order to manage potential drug‑drug interactions and ensure safe patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1369
retrieved_docs.content: The human cytochrome P450 3A4 protein metabolizes the chemotherapeutic agent paclitaxel and the proton‑pump inhibitor omeprazol, indicating that CYP3A4 activity is a key determinant of the clearance of these drugs. Amiodaron is also a substrate of the same CYP3A4 enzyme, so its plasma levels are influenced by factors that induce or inhibit CYP3A4. In addition, amiodaron acts as an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 isoform CYP2C9, which can diminish the metabolic capacity of CYP2C9 for other co‑administered medications. Consequently, drugs that are metabolized by CYP2C9 may achieve higher concentrations when given with amiodaron, while agents that affect CYP3A4 can alter amiodaron exposure. Recognizing these interconnected metabolic pathways is essential for anticipating drug‑drug interactions and optimizing patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1794
retrieved_docs.content: Cytochrome P450 3A4 inhibitors are identified as having a contraindicated mechanism of action when combined with finerenone, indicating that finerenone should be avoided in patients receiving CYP3A4‑inhibiting drugs. Conivaptan itself acts through inhibition of the Cytochrome P450 3A4 enzyme, making it a CYP3A4 inhibitor, and this pharmacologic action produces the physiologic effect of increased diuresis. Because of its mechanism, conivaptan is contraindicated in individuals with urinary retention (انقطاع البول). Similarly, bisoprolol is also contraindicated in the presence of urinary retention, highlighting the need to assess bladder function before prescribing either conivaptan or bisoprolol. Together, these relationships underscore important drug‑interaction and safety considerations for finerenone, conivaptan, and bisoprolol in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2102
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodarone (spelled “amiodaron” in the source data) is a substrate of the human cytochrome P450 3A4 enzyme, which means CYP3A4 metabolizes amiodarone as it does other drugs such as omeprazole. In addition, amiodarone functions as an inhibitor of cytochrome P450 enzymes CYP2C9 and the CYP3A family (including CYP3A4). Consequently, amiodarone can both be cleared by CYP3A4 and simultaneously reduce the metabolic activity of CYP2C9 and CYP3A enzymes, potentially altering the plasma concentrations of co‑administered medications that depend on these pathways. This dual role as a substrate and inhibitor is important for clinicians to consider when prescribing amiodarone together with other drugs metabolized by CYP2C9 or CYP3A4, such as omeprazole, to avoid adverse drug interactions and ensure optimal patient care.
